infobox.value: is a musical project, typically with looser constraints than that of an [album](/wiki/Album "Album") or [extended play](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play"). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Musical project

For video, see [Video mixtape](/wiki/Video_mixtape "Video mixtape"). For other uses, see [Mixtape (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mixtape_(disambiguation) "Mixtape (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Lil_Wayne_%2823513397583%29.jpg/250px-Lil_Wayne_%2823513397583%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lil_Wayne_(23513397583).jpg)

Artist [Lil Wayne](/wiki/Lil_Wayne "Lil Wayne"), who has a total of [29 mixtapes](/wiki/Lil_Wayne_albums_discography "Lil Wayne albums discography"), is regarded as one of the most successful mixtape artists.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In the modern music industry, a **mixtape** is a musical project, typically with looser constraints than that of an [album](/wiki/Album "Album") or [extended play](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play"). Unlike the traditional album or extended play, mixtapes are labeled as laid-back projects that allow artists more creative freedom and less commercial pressure.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The term has significantly increased in popularity over the years due to high-profile artists marketing their projects as such.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)
Prior to the decline of [physical media](/wiki/Physical_media "Physical media"), mixtapes were defined as homemade compilations of music played through a [cassette tape](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape"), [CD](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc"), or digital [playlist](/wiki/Playlist "Playlist") and became significant in [hip-hop culture](/wiki/Hip-hop_culture "Hip-hop culture"). The songs were typically [beatmatched](/wiki/Beatmatching "Beatmatching") and consisted of seamless transitions at their beginnings and endings with fades or abrupt edits.[[5]](#cite_note-5) It was then defined as relatively any musical project by an up-and-coming artist. Now, mixtapes have become a label of promotion and marketing for album-like projects.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) Dictionary.com writes that "the line between an album and a mixtape can be blurry", and that streaming services rely on the artist to make that determination.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Cassette mixtapes (1970s–1990s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cassette mixtapes (1970s–1990s)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Funky_Stuff_mixtape.jpg/250px-Funky_Stuff_mixtape.jpg)](/wiki/File:Funky_Stuff_mixtape.jpg)

A compact [audio cassette](/wiki/Audio_cassette "Audio cassette") mixtape with a handwritten label: "Funky Stuff"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/8trackmixtape.JPG/250px-8trackmixtape.JPG)](/wiki/File:8trackmixtape.JPG)

An early pirated [8-track](/wiki/8-track_cartridge "8-track cartridge") mixtape from 1974

The idea of mixtapes was conceived in the early 1970s with the genesis of [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"). In the 1970s, [DJs](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey") such as [Grandmaster Flash](/wiki/Grandmaster_Flash "Grandmaster Flash") and the [Furious Five](/wiki/Furious_Five "Furious Five"), [Afrika Bambaataa](/wiki/Afrika_Bambaataa "Afrika Bambaataa"), and [DJ Hollywood](/wiki/DJ_Hollywood "DJ Hollywood") would often distribute recordings of their club performances through cassette tapes, bringing a wider audience to the hip-hop sound.[[8]](#cite_note-8) These cassettes eventually became colloquially known as mixtapes. The introduction of the [Sony Walkman](/wiki/Walkman "Walkman") in 1979 saw major improvements in the mobility and practicality of mixtapes.[[9]](#cite_note-:5-9)

In the 1980s and 1990s, mixtapes evolved as recordings of exclusive tracks, freestyles, and remixes. They also started becoming a visible element of [youth culture](/wiki/Youth_culture "Youth culture").[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) This blended both the underground and commercial appeal of mixtapes together. A notable development in mixtapes at this time was the "[chopped and screwed](/wiki/Chopped_and_screwed "Chopped and screwed")" technique created by [DJ Screw](/wiki/DJ_Screw "DJ Screw") in [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas").[[9]](#cite_note-:5-9) This technique created a slow-tempo sound for mixtapes, later becoming a staple of [Southern hip-hop](/wiki/Southern_hip_hop "Southern hip hop"). Another development of the mixtape were the creations of the [remix](/wiki/Remix "Remix") and the [mashup](/wiki/Mashup_(music) "Mashup (music)"), which gave pre-existing songs a new sound to their original counterpart.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the 1990s, mixtapes eventually moved to [CD burners](/wiki/Optical_disc_drive "Optical disc drive") and [MP3 players](/wiki/MP3_players "MP3 players") as the cassette tape declined. The curation of mixtapes became more intimate as well, as many curators would assemble songs in their tapes as an overarching [theme](/wiki/Theme_(narrative) "Theme (narrative)") that they could send to their audience.

### Redefining the mixtape (2000s–2010s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Redefining the mixtape (2000s–2010s)")]

In the 2000s, mixtapes transitioned from physical media to a synonym for unofficial albums that may have legal issues being officially released.[[12]](#cite_note-12) They also became significant to developing artist recognition. Artists like [50 Cent](/wiki/50_Cent "50 Cent") used mixtapes to build their reputation before being signed to a [label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label").[[13]](#cite_note-13) This also allowed for more artistry as mixtapes were not confined by the legal restrictions of a record label. As a result of his mixtapes' success, he released his critically acclaimed album *[Get Rich or Die Tryin'](/wiki/Get_Rich_or_Die_Tryin%27 "Get Rich or Die Tryin'")*.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

By the mid- to late 2000s, the original definition of the mixtape in the 1970s seemed to vanish; it instead became the term for any musical project to promote rising artists.[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) The creation of the streaming platform [DatPiff](/wiki/DatPiff "DatPiff") introduced the publishing of mixtapes online and for free, which made mixtapes easier to obtain.[[15]](#cite_note-:7-15)

In 2015, Canadian rapper [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)") released his mixtape *[If You're Reading This It's Too Late](/wiki/If_You%27re_Reading_This_It%27s_Too_Late "If You're Reading This It's Too Late")*, significantly muddling the line between mixtapes and albums, setting the precedent that an album-like project could be promoted as a mixtape.[[15]](#cite_note-:7-15)

### Streaming era and the modern mixtape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Streaming era and the modern mixtape")]

The popularization of [streaming platforms](/wiki/Over-the-top_media_service "Over-the-top media service") and the rapid decline of physical media such as [CDs](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") and [cassettes](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape") have significantly altered the definition of a mixtape and rendered the physical mixtape obsolete. Today, mixtapes are generally considered an alternative to studio albums, especially in genres of hip-hop, [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), and [indie](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") music.[[16]](#cite_note-:4-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) They allow artists to release music without industry-level expectations expected from the likes of a [concept album](/wiki/Concept_album "Concept album").[[18]](#cite_note-:6-18) Mixtapes have become staples in the music industry and are sometimes released as holdovers or low-key releases between [studio albums](/wiki/Studio_album "Studio album").[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) Lesser-known artists may release them free online on more accessible streaming platforms like [SoundCloud](/wiki/SoundCloud "SoundCloud") to gain exposure, while well-known industry artists who release mixtapes usually promote them as "commercial mixtapes" as they are released on profitable streaming platforms such as [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") or [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music").[[19]](#cite_note-:9-19) Notable examples of commercial mixtapes include *[Street Gossip](/wiki/Street_Gossip_(mixtape) "Street Gossip (mixtape)")* by [Lil Baby](/wiki/Lil_Baby "Lil Baby"), *[MMM (Money Making Mitch)](/wiki/MMM_(Money_Making_Mitch) "MMM (Money Making Mitch)")* by [Puff Daddy](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs"), and *[Dark Lane Demo Tapes](/wiki/Dark_Lane_Demo_Tapes "Dark Lane Demo Tapes")* by [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-:9-19)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### Current trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Current trends")]

While the traditional album might stick to one theme, mixtapes have allowed artists to present more diverse music, atypical from the conventional concept album.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In an era of short-form content, mixtapes are valuable in that they give room to maintain relevance and adapt to the fast pace of [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media"). Because of the flexibility that mixtapes allow, releasing them on platforms like [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok") can instantly increase an artist's mainstream recognition and reach a broader audience, and of course, without the expectations of a full album rollout.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Release and marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Release and marketing")]

While mixtapes used to be distributed for free, [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music"), and [SoundCloud](/wiki/SoundCloud "SoundCloud") have blurred the line between what is considered a full-on album versus what is considered a mixtape.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

However, artists typically distinguish an album from a mixtape in two ways:[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Faces_sandwich.jpg/250px-Faces_sandwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Faces_sandwich.jpg)

Atypical promotion for the mixtape [*Faces*](/wiki/Faces_(mixtape) "Faces (mixtape)") by [Mac Miller](/wiki/Mac_Miller "Mac Miller"). People downloading the mixtape would have to make their own sandwich before being able to listen.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Marketing")]

The marketing of a mixtape is usually minimal, spontaneous, and unorthodox. In contrast, studio albums often have more professional media campaigns, [music videos](/wiki/Music_video "Music video") and a set release. Because of a mixtape's "unpredictability" that varies from artist, mixtapes may entertain fans with excitement due to surprise releases.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

### Digital release and promotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Digital release and promotion")]

On streaming platforms, mixtapes are nearly identical in format, as both include album art, titles, and track lists. However, mixtapes may lack the proper promotion and radio play that traditional albums offer. Artists may informally promote their mixtapes by posting to their own social media (as opposed to having a management team do it) or by directly engaging with their fans online.[[28]](#cite_note-:3-28)

## Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Purpose")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Travi%24_Scott_2015.jpg/250px-Travi%24_Scott_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Travi$_Scott_2015.jpg)

Rapper [Travis Scott](/wiki/Travis_Scott "Travis Scott") performing his mixtape at a concert in anticipation for his album.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

For independent and emerging artists, mixtapes are a gateway to building a fanbase and experimenting with their sound.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) For established artists, mixtapes can be an outlet for personal expression and experimentation to escape the pressures of a [record label](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") or commercial appeal.[[18]](#cite_note-:6-18)[[28]](#cite_note-:3-28)[[30]](#cite_note-30) The absence of formal promotion, industry-standard production, or chart performance makes mixtapes often feature more raw and experimental sounds, which may be preferable to their fans.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) The value of a mixtape lies in its ability to define the artist rather than an industry standard, making it an important concept in music.[[16]](#cite_note-:4-16)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

A mixtape can also serve as a precursor to an upcoming album for an artist. As an example, [Travis Scott](/wiki/Travis_Scott "Travis Scott") released his mixtape *[Days Before Rodeo](/wiki/Days_Before_Rodeo "Days Before Rodeo")* as an anticipation project for his debut album, *[Rodeo](/wiki/Rodeo_(Travis_Scott_album) "Rodeo (Travis Scott album)")*.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Another example is *[While We Wait](/wiki/While_We_Wait "While We Wait")* by singer and songwriter [Kehlani](/wiki/Kehlani "Kehlani"). The name of the tape highlights the informal circumstances of the mixtape, which was released in interim to give her fans music to listen to before the release of her album *[It Was Good Until It Wasn't](/wiki/It_Was_Good_Until_It_Wasn%27t "It Was Good Until It Wasn't")*.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

## Legal issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mixtape&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Legal issues")]

Many well-known mixtapes are ineligible to be released on streaming platforms due to [sample clearance issues](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") or any [licensing issues](/wiki/Music_licensing "Music licensing").[[33]](#cite_note-33) A well-known example is *[Nostalgia, Ultra](/wiki/Nostalgia,_Ultra "Nostalgia, Ultra")* by [Frank Ocean](/wiki/Frank_Ocean "Frank Ocean").[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

However, some mixtapes have overcome their sample clearances, allowing them to be released on major streaming platforms. These include *[Acid Rap](/wiki/Acid_Rap "Acid Rap")* by [Chance the Rapper](/wiki/Chance_the_Rapper "Chance the Rapper"), *[Live. Love. ASAP](/wiki/Live._Love._ASAP "Live. Love. ASAP")* by [ASAP Rocky](/wiki/ASAP_Rocky "ASAP Rocky"), *[So Far Gone](/wiki/So_Far_Gone_(mixtape) "So Far Gone (mixtape)")* by [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)") and *[Friday Night Lights](/wiki/Friday_Night_Lights_(mixtape) "Friday Night Lights (mixtape)")* by [J. Cole](/wiki/J._Cole "J. Cole").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)
